claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right
circumstances: Child is 18 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child receives qualifying benefits (e.g. UC, ESA) in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - my son Alex. He's 18 years old and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to understand Alex's current situation to determine eligibility.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he studying at school, college, or in an approved training program?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's still in education. He's doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. A-levels at sixth form college would typically count as approved education.

**Does Alex have any paid work? If so, how many hours per week does he work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't have a job. He's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's good to know. Now I need to understand your living situation with Alex.

**Does Alex live with you, or does he live elsewhere?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. One final question:

**Does Alex receive any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, actually he does receive Universal Credit in his own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Eligibility Decision for Alex

I'm sorry, but **you are not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Here's why:**

While Alex meets several of the criteria that would normally allow Child Benefit to continue:
- He's 18 years old (under the age 20 limit)
- He's in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- He lives with you
- He doesn't work 24 hours or more per week

Unfortunately, according to the Child Benefit rules, you stop being eligible for Child Benefit if your child starts receiving certain benefits in their own right. Since Alex receives **Universal Credit** in his own name, this disqualifies you from claiming Child Benefit for him, even though he's still in education.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he stops receiving Universal Credit), you may want to reconsider your eligibility at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. He lives with you and does not work 24 hours or more per week. However, according to the rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit. Since Alex receives Universal Credit in his own name, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for him, even though he is still in approved education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with their 18-year-old son Alex, who is studying A-levels at sixth form college and does not have paid employment. While Alex would normally qualify for Child Benefit as he is under 20 and in approved education, the fact that he receives Universal Credit in his own name disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for him, as per the rules which state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts getting certain benefits including Universal Credit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and is in approved education (A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally allow Child Benefit to continue until age 20. He lives with you and does not work 24 hours or more per week. However, according to the rules, you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts getting certain benefits, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit. Since Alex receives Universal Credit in his own name, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for him, even though he is still in approved education.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester) with their 18-year-old son Alex, who is studying A-levels at sixth form college and does not have paid employment. While Alex would normally qualify for Child Benefit as he is under 20 and in approved education, the fact that he receives Universal Credit in his own name disqualifies the claimant from receiving Child Benefit for him, as per the rules which state that Child Benefit stops when a child starts getting certain benefits including Universal Credit.
